name: Quantum Foundations
description: Measurement, entanglement, no-go theorems, quantum information foundations

results.name: Bell's Theorem
results.structural_conclusion: Local hidden variable theories cannot reproduce all quantum mechanical predictions, revealing that quantum correlations transcend classical locality constraints.

results.name: Kochen-Specker Theorem
results.structural_conclusion: Quantum observables cannot possess predetermined values independent of measurement context, establishing the fundamentally contextual nature of quantum properties.
results.source_citation: Kochen, S. & Specker, E.P. (1967). J. Math. Mech., 17, 59-87

results.name: No-Cloning Theorem
results.structural_conclusion: Arbitrary quantum states cannot be perfectly duplicated, revealing a fundamental constraint on quantum information that distinguishes it from classical information.

results.name: CHSH Inequality
results.structural_conclusion: Provides experimentally testable bounds on correlations in local realistic theories, which quantum mechanics violates up to the Tsirelson bound.
results.source_citation: Clauser, J.F. et al. (1969). Phys. Rev. Lett., 23, 880-884

results.name: Aspect's Experiments
results.structural_conclusion: Experimental violation of Bell inequalities confirms that nature exhibits non-local correlations incompatible with local realism.
results.source_citation: Aspect, A., Dalibard, J. & Roger, G. (1982). Phys. Rev. Lett., 49, 1804-1807

results.name: Quantum Teleportation Protocol
results.structural_conclusion: Quantum states can be transmitted using only classical communication and pre-shared entanglement, revealing entanglement as a fundamental communication resource.
results.source_citation: Bennett, C.H. et al. (1993). Phys. Rev. Lett., 70, 1895-1899

results.name: Decoherence Theory
results.structural_conclusion: Environmental entanglement dynamically selects preferred pointer states, explaining the emergence of classical behavior through information dispersal rather than collapse.

results.name: PBR Theorem
results.structural_conclusion: Quantum states cannot be interpreted as states of knowledge about an underlying reality, establishing their ontological rather than purely epistemic character.
results.source_citation: Pusey, M.F., Barrett, J. & Rudolph, T. (2012). Nature Physics, 8, 475-478

results.name: Gleason's Theorem
results.structural_conclusion: The Born rule is the unique probability measure on quantum observables in dimensions ≥3, constraining possible modifications to quantum theory.

results.name: Monogamy of Entanglement
results.structural_conclusion: Quantum entanglement cannot be freely shared among multiple parties, imposing fundamental constraints on multipartite quantum correlations.
results.source_citation: Coffman, V., Kundu, J. & Wootters, W.K. (2000). Phys. Rev. A, 61, 052306

results.name: Quantum Zeno Effect
results.structural_conclusion: Frequent measurements can freeze quantum evolution, revealing measurement as an active process that fundamentally alters system dynamics.
results.source_citation: Misra, B. & Sudarshan, E.C.G. (1977). J. Math. Phys., 18, 756-763

results.name: Hardy's Paradox
results.structural_conclusion: Demonstrates quantum non-locality without inequalities, showing that local realism fails even for individual measurement outcomes.

results.name: Quantum Darwinism
results.structural_conclusion: Classical objectivity emerges through redundant encoding of selected information in the environment, explaining the transition from quantum to classical.

results.name: Free Will Theorem
results.structural_conclusion: If experimenters have free will, then elementary particles must also possess it, linking quantum indeterminacy to fundamental questions of agency.
results.source_citation: Conway, J. & Kochen, S. (2006). Found. Phys., 36, 1441-1473

results.name: Tsirelson's Bound
results.structural_conclusion: Quantum correlations are bounded below hypothetical super-quantum correlations, suggesting quantum mechanics occupies a special position in the space of possible theories.

results.name: Quantum Error Correction Threshold Theorem
results.structural_conclusion: Quantum computation can be made arbitrarily reliable despite decoherence if error rates fall below a threshold, establishing the feasibility of scalable quantum computing.
results.source_citation: Aharonov, D. & Ben-Or, M. (1997). Proc. 29th STOC, 176-188

structural_conclusion: Quantum foundations reveals that reality's fundamental structure violates classical intuitions about locality, realism, and information. Quantum properties are irreducibly contextual, non-local correlations cannot be explained by hidden variables, and information obeys fundamentally different rules than classical bits. The quantum-classical transition emerges through environmental entanglement and information proliferation rather than fundamental collapse.
